# Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Uncertainty is an inescapable feature of knowledge, decision-making, and
communication across virtually every domain of human endeavour. A physician
advising a patient on the likely progression of a disease, a meteorologist
issuing a five-day forecast, an economist projecting next year's growth, a
hydrologist modelling flood risk, an intelligence analyst assessing the
likelihood of a geopolitical event — all are working with incomplete,
imperfect, or inherently variable information. In each case, how that
uncertainty is understood, framed, and communicated to those who must act on
it can have consequences as significant as the underlying knowledge itself.

Yet despite its ubiquity, uncertainty is frequently treated as an
inconvenience to be minimised or hidden rather than a property of knowledge to
be honestly represented. Point estimates are reported without ranges. Forecasts
are presented as predictions. Probabilistic language is avoided for fear of
appearing indecisive or undermining confidence. The result is a systematic
mismatch between what is actually known and what is communicated — one that
can mislead decision-makers, erode public trust, and contribute to poor
outcomes. As @fischhoff2014 argue, unless uncertainty is communicated
effectively, decision makers may place too much or too little faith in the
information they receive, with potentially serious consequences. The principle
is stated simply by @nrc2006: no forecast is complete without a description of
its uncertainty.

The challenge is compounded by a fundamental tension identified across the
literature. Those who generate uncertain information — forecasters, scientists,
analysts — frequently prefer to communicate it in vague, qualitative terms,
partly because doing so reduces their accountability if a prediction turns out
to be wrong [@dhami2022]. Those who consume uncertain information — the public,
policymakers, patients, risk managers — typically prefer precise, numerical
communication, because they need concrete boundaries to make high-stakes
decisions [@dhami2022]. This sender-receiver mismatch is one of the most
persistent and practically consequential problems in the field.

Communicating uncertainty is also genuinely difficult. Audiences differ in
their statistical literacy, their tolerance for ambiguity, and their
preferences for how information is presented. Methods that work well for expert
audiences — such as probability distributions or quantile intervals — may
confuse or alienate non-specialist readers. Simpler formats, such as
categorical ratings or verbal expressions, risk oversimplifying or introducing
their own forms of misunderstanding. There is no universally best method; what
works depends on the message, the medium, and the audience. Furthermore, as
@vanderbles2019 demonstrate, uncertainty lacks a single agreed definition
across disciplines, complicating both the production and reception of
uncertainty communication at a fundamental level.

This paper aims to serve as a practical, cross-disciplinary reference for
anyone who needs to communicate uncertainty — whether in a scientific report, a
policy document, a data visualisation, a public health bulletin, or a financial
forecast. It is written to be accessible to readers from any discipline, and
draws on literature spanning statistics, meteorology, climate science,
economics, medicine, psychology, intelligence analysis, ecology, and risk
communication. The paper does not attempt to survey the full literature on
uncertainty quantification, probabilistic modelling, or decision theory — each
of these is a vast field in its own right. The focus is squarely on
*communication*: how uncertain information is represented and conveyed to an
audience, and how different representational choices affect understanding, trust,
and decision-making.

## Structure of This Paper

The paper is organised as follows. @sec-conceptualising introduces uncertainty
as a concept, reviewing key definitions and frameworks from across the
literature, including the distinction between epistemic and aleatory
uncertainty, Stirling's four-dimensional matrix of incertitude, and the
concepts of deep uncertainty and unknown unknowns. @sec-sources examines where
uncertainty comes from, identifying five major categories of source: measurement
and data uncertainty, model uncertainty, parameter uncertainty, structural and
scenario uncertainty, and human and judgmental uncertainty. @sec-related
situates uncertainty among four closely related concepts — variability,
volatility, randomness, and risk — clarifying how these differ and why the
distinctions matter for communication. @sec-why asks why uncertainty should be
communicated at all, examines the consequences of failing to do so, and
considers the range of audiences who may need to engage with uncertain
information, from technical experts to the general public. The core of the
paper is @sec-methods, which reviews eight distinct approaches to communicating
uncertainty: forecast trajectories, fan charts, probability distributions,
prediction intervals and quantiles, categorical approaches, scenario and what-if
analysis, verbal expressions, and numerical expressions. For each method, the
paper discusses what it is, provides a practical example with a figure
placeholder, examines its strengths and limitations, identifies when it is most
appropriate, and offers good practice recommendations. Finally, @sec-conclusion
draws together cross-cutting principles, presents a summary table matching
methods to audience types, and identifies open questions for future work.

## How to Use This Paper

This paper is designed to be read as a whole, but it can equally be used as a
reference in which individual sections are consulted as needed. Readers who are
new to the topic may wish to read Sections [-@sec-conceptualising] through
[-@sec-why] before turning to the methods. Practitioners who already have a
working understanding of uncertainty and are seeking guidance on communication
methods may proceed directly to @sec-methods. Each method subsection in
@sec-methods is self-contained, with cross-references to the conceptual
sections where relevant.

::: {.callout-note}
**A note on scope:** This paper focuses on the communication of *quantitative*
or *semi-quantitative* uncertainty — uncertainty that can be expressed, at least
in part, through numbers, ranges, probabilities, or calibrated language. The
communication of purely qualitative uncertainty, philosophical scepticism, and
the sociological and political dimensions of uncertainty governance, while
touched upon where relevant, are not the primary focus.
:::
